title: Half-Edge Data Structure. Part 2
excerpt: In the previous post we covered the basics of the half-edge data structure and how to traverse its elements using OpenMesh. This time, we’ll look under the hood of add_face – the functi…
content_html:
In the previous post we covered the basics of the half-edge data structure and how to traverse its elements using OpenMesh. This time, we’ll look under the hood of add_face – the function that builds the entire structure on the fly. When you call:

OpenMesh performs a sequence of operations to wire up the entire topological graph. Let’s trace through what happens step by step.

Imagine two triangles forming a quad, and a third triangle attached to its right side. Let’s start with simple quad described in Post 1.

And add a third triangle attached to its right side.

Before F2 is added, the boundary loop of the quad is closed and contains four halfedges:

h1(v1, v0)
-> h9(v0, v3)
-> h7(v3, v2)
-> h3(v2, v1)
-> h1

Now watch what happens if we add F2 without updating the boundary links.

Suppose we create the two missing edges – four new halfedges, h10 through h13 – assign the new face, and close its inner contour. The existing halfedge h3 now continues into h10.

Try walking the old boundary again:

h1(v1, v0)
-> h9(v0, v3)
-> h7(v3, v2)
-> h3(v2, v1)
-> h10(v1, v4)
-> h12(v4, v2)
-> h3
-> h10
-> ...

The walk never returns to h1.

We fall through h3 into the new face and orbit there forever. The boundary loop is broken.

Adding a face is therefore not simply a matter of creating the missing edges. The halfedge links around the seam must also be repaired, carefully and in the correct order. That is what most of the work inside add_face() is about.

The new edges brought not only h10 and h12 – the halfedges the face itself consumes – but also their twins: h11 (v4,v1) and h13 (v2,v4). Two fresh boundary halfedges with no face on them yet. The fix is to route the boundary loop through them instead of h3: h7 should lead into h13, h13 into h11, and h11 back into h1. Three pointer writes, and the walk closes again – h1 -> h9 -> h7 -> h13 -> h11 -> h1 – with the boundary now going around the new triangle.

Now add Face 2 using the existing edge E1 and the newly created edges E5 and E6, and update the affected halfedge links.

Computing next

But how do we compute these next pointers?

The invariant of the structure is that next(h) must be the next halfedge in the same cycle – either a face contour or a boundary loop – and it must start where h ends.

Therefore, to repair a boundary halfedge, we can stand at its end vertex and rotate through the outgoing halfedges until we find another boundary one:

Before running this algorithm, the inner contour of the new face must already be valid:

and all three halfedges must belong to F2.

Now run fix_next for h7, which ends at v2.

The outgoing halfedges at v2, in rotational order, are:

The search starts at:

Since h6 belongs to F1, it continues with:

h4 belongs to F0, so it rotates again:

But h3 now belongs to the newly added face F2, so the rotation continues once more:

h13 is a boundary halfedge, so the search stops:

Notice what changed. Before F2 was added, h3 was still a boundary halfedge, so the rotation stopped there:

The new face consumed that old boundary halfedge. The same rotation now passes through it and reaches the next free outgoing halfedge, h13.

The same rule produces the other two boundary links.

For h13, which ends at the new vertex v4, the first outgoing candidate is:

h12 belongs to F2, so one rotation gives:

h11 is boundary, therefore:

There are only two outgoing halfedges at the new vertex v4, h12 and h11, so the rotation simply passes through the inner one and reaches the outer one.

Finally, consider h11, which ends at v1.

The rotation starts at:

h10 belongs to F2, so the next candidate is:

h2 belongs to F0, so we rotate again:

h1 is boundary, giving:

All three required writes therefore follow from the same general rule:

The repaired boundary loop is:

The OpenMesh solution

For this common case, OpenMesh does not need to perform these rotations. The answers are already encoded in the old boundary loop.

Before inserting F2, the existing halfedge h3 was part of the boundary:

Therefore:

When h3 becomes part of the new face, its two new boundary replacements are already known:

OpenMesh can therefore construct the new boundary using direct pointer operations:

Substituting the actual handles gives:

These are exactly the same links that the general fix_next algorithm found by rotating around the affected vertices.

There is one important catch: prev(h3) and next(h3) must be read before the old boundary links are overwritten.

For this reason, add_face does not apply each change immediately. Instead, it stores the planned assignments in next_cache_:

Only after all old links have been inspected does OpenMesh apply the entire batch:

Conceptually, fix_next rebuilds boundary connectivity by searching:

OpenMesh usually reaches the same result through local splice operations:

The more complicated re-link patches branch handles a different situation: two consecutive sides of the new face already exist as boundary halfedges, but they are not consecutive in the current boundary loop.

In that case, a whole boundary patch may lie between them. OpenMesh rotates around the shared vertex to find another free boundary gap, moves the patch into that gap, and then links the two reused halfedges directly.

This rotation follows the same underlying boundary invariant as fix_next, but it performs a larger patch-splicing operation rather than repairing only one next pointer.

Why adjust_outgoing_halfedge() is still needed

At the end, adjust_outgoing_halfedge() checks whether a vertex still points to a suitable outgoing halfedge. After adding a face, the halfedge stored by a boundary vertex may now belong to the new face and no longer be part of the boundary. The function rotates around the vertex, finds another outgoing boundary halfedge if one exists, and stores it as the vertex’s new representative. It does not change the mesh topology – it only fixes the vertex’s entry point for future traversal.

Summary

Conceptually, the function performs three main jobs:

The conceptual fix_next rule explains which boundary halfedges must be connected. OpenMesh usually avoids performing the full rotational search and instead computes the same links directly from the old boundary structure. It stores those changes in next_cache_, applies them only after all required reads are complete, and finally calls adjust_outgoing_halfedge() for any vertex whose stored traversal entry point is no longer suitable.

This time, we’ll look under the hood of add_face – the function that builds the entire structure on the fly. When you call: OpenMesh performs a sequence of operations to wire up the entire topological graph. Let’s trace through what happens step by step. Imagine two triangles forming a quad, and a third triangle attached to its right side. Let’s start with simple quad described in Post 1. And add a third triangle attached to its right side. Before F2 is added, the boundary loop of the quad is closed and contains four halfedges: h1(v1, v0) -> h9(v0, v3) -> h7(v3, v2) -> h3(v2, v1) -> h1 Now watch what happens if we add F2 without updating the boundary links. Suppose we create the two missing edges – four new halfedges, h10 through h13 – assign the new face, and close its inner contour. The existing halfedge h3 now continues into h10. Try walking the old boundary again: h1(v1, v0) -> h9(v0, v3) -> h7(v3, v2) -> h3(v2, v1) -> h10(v1, v4) -> h12(v4, v2) -> h3 -> h10 -> ... The walk never returns to h1. We fall through h3 into the new face and orbit there forever. The boundary loop is broken. Adding a face is therefore not simply a matter of creating the missing edges. The halfedge links around the seam must also be repaired, carefully and in the correct order. That is what most of the work inside add_face() is about. The new edges brought not only h10 and h12 – the halfedges the face itself consumes – but also their twins: h11 (v4,v1) and h13 (v2,v4). Two fresh boundary halfedges with no face on them yet. The fix is to route the boundary loop through them instead of h3: h7 should lead into h13, h13 into h11, and h11 back into h1. Three pointer writes, and the walk closes again – h1 -> h9 -> h7 -> h13 -> h11 -> h1 – with the boundary now going around the new triangle. Now add Face 2 using the existing edge E1 and the newly created edges E5 and E6, and update the affected halfedge links. Computing next But how do we compute these next pointers? The invariant of the structure is that next(h) must be the next halfedge in the same cycle – either a face contour or a boundary loop – and it must start where h ends. Therefore, to repair a boundary halfedge, we can stand at its end vertex and rotate through the outgoing halfedges until we find another boundary one: Before running this algorithm, the inner contour of the new face must already be valid: and all three halfedges must belong to F2. Now run fix_next for h7, which ends at v2. The outgoing halfedges at v2, in rotational order, are: The search starts at: Since h6 belongs to F1, it continues with: h4 belongs to F0, so it rotates again: But h3 now belongs to the newly added face F2, so the rotation continues once more: h13 is a boundary halfedge, so the search stops: Notice what changed. Before F2 was added, h3 was still a boundary halfedge, so the rotation stopped there: The new face consumed that old boundary halfedge. The same rotation now passes through it and reaches the next free outgoing halfedge, h13. The same rule produces the other two boundary links. For h13, which ends at the new vertex v4, the first outgoing candidate is: h12 belongs to F2, so one rotation gives: h11 is boundary, therefore: There are only two outgoing halfedges at the new vertex v4, h12 and h11, so the rotation simply passes through the inner one and reaches the outer one. Finally, consider h11, which ends at v1. The rotation starts at: h10 belongs to F2, so the next candidate is: h2 belongs to F0, so we rotate again: h1 is boundary, giving: All three required writes therefore follow from the same general rule: The repaired boundary loop is: The OpenMesh solution For this common case, OpenMesh does not need to perform these rotations. The answers are already encoded in the old boundary loop. Before inserting F2, the existing halfedge h3 was part of the boundary: Therefore: When h3 becomes part of the new face, its two new boundary replacements are already known: OpenMesh can therefore construct the new boundary using direct pointer operations: Substituting the actual handles gives: These are exactly the same links that the general fix_next algorithm found by rotating around the affected vertices. There is one important catch: prev(h3) and next(h3) must be read before the old boundary links are overwritten. For this reason, add_face does not apply each change immediately. Instead, it stores the planned assignments in next_cache_: Only after all old links have been inspected does OpenMesh apply the entire batch: Conceptually, fix_next rebuilds boundary connectivity by searching: OpenMesh usually reaches the same result through local splice operations: The more complicated re-link patches branch handles a different situation: two consecutive sides of the new face already exist as boundary halfedges, but they are not consecutive in the current boundary loop. In that case, a whole boundary patch may lie between them. OpenMesh rotates around the shared vertex to find another free boundary gap, moves the patch into that gap, and then links the two reused halfedges directly. This rotation follows the same underlying boundary invariant as fix_next, but it performs a larger patch-splicing operation rather than repairing only one next pointer. After adding a face, the halfedge stored by a boundary vertex may now belong to the new face and no longer be part of the boundary. The function rotates around the vertex, finds another outgoing boundary halfedge if one exists, and stores it as the vertex’s new representative. It does not change the mesh topology – it only fixes the vertex’s entry point for future traversal. Summary Conceptually, the function performs three main jobs: The conceptual fix_next rule explains which boundary halfedges must be connected. OpenMesh usually avoids performing the full rotational search and instead computes the same links directly from the old boundary structure. It stores those changes in next_cache_, applies them only after all required reads are complete, and finally calls adjust_outgoing_halfedge() for any vertex whose stored traversal entry point is no longer suitable.

sentences.en: In the previous post we covered the basics of the half-edge data structure and how to traverse its elements using OpenMesh.
sentences.zh: 在上一篇文章中，我们介绍了半边数据结构的基础知识以及如何使用 OpenMesh 遍历其元素。

sentences.en: This time, we’ll look under the hood of add_face – the function that builds the entire structure on the fly.
sentences.zh: 这次，我们将深入了解 add_face 的底层——该函数动态构建整个结构。

sentences.en: OpenMesh performs a sequence of operations to wire up the entire topological graph.
sentences.zh: OpenMesh 执行一系列操作来连接整个拓扑图。

sentences.en: Let’s trace through what happens step by step.
sentences.zh: 让我们一步步追踪到底发生了什么。

sentences.en: Let’s start with simple quad described in Post 1.
sentences.zh: 让我们从第 1 篇文章中描述的简单四边形开始。

blocks.html_en: And add a third triangle attached to its right side.

sentences.en: And add a third triangle attached to its right side.
sentences.zh: 并添加第三个三角形连接到其右侧。

blocks.html_en: Before F2 is added, the boundary loop of the quad is closed and contains four halfedges:

blocks.html_en:
h1(v1, v0)
-> h9(v0, v3)
-> h7(v3, v2)
-> h3(v2, v1)
-> h1
Now watch what happens if we add F2 without updating the boundary links.

sentences.en: Now watch what happens if we add F2 without updating the boundary links.
sentences.zh: 现在观察如果我们添加 F2 而不更新边界链接会发生什么。

blocks.html_en: Suppose we create the two missing edges – four new halfedges, h10 through h13 – assign the new face, and close its inner contour. The existing halfedge h3 now continues into h10.

sentences.zh: 假设我们创建两条缺失的边——四个新的半边，h10 到 h13——分配新面，并闭合其内部轮廓。

sentences.en: The existing halfedge h3 now continues into h10.
sentences.zh: 现有的 halfedge h3 现在延续到 h10。

blocks.html_en: Try walking the old boundary again:

sentences.en: Try walking the old boundary again:
sentences.zh: 再次尝试沿着旧边界行走：

blocks.html_en: The walk never returns to h1.

sentences.en: The walk never returns to h1.
sentences.zh: 步行永远不会返回到 h1。

blocks.html_en: We fall through h3 into the new face and orbit there forever. The boundary loop is broken.

sentences.en: We fall through h3 into the new face and orbit there forever.
sentences.zh: 我们穿过 h3 进入新的面孔并永远围绕那里运行。

sentences.en: The boundary loop is broken.
sentences.zh: 边界环被破坏。

blocks.html_en: Adding a face is therefore not simply a matter of creating the missing edges. The halfedge links around the seam must also be repaired, carefully and in the correct order. That is what most of the work inside add_face() is about.

sentences.en: Adding a face is therefore not simply a matter of creating the missing edges.
sentences.zh: 因此，添加面不仅仅是创建缺失边缘的问题。

sentences.en: The halfedge links around the seam must also be repaired, carefully and in the correct order.
sentences.zh: 接缝周围的半边链接也必须按照正确的顺序仔细修复。

sentences.en: That is what most of the work inside add_face() is about.
sentences.zh: 这就是 add_face() 内部大部分工作的内容。

blocks.html_en: The new edges brought not only h10 and h12 – the halfedges the face itself consumes – but also their twins: h11 (v4,v1) and h13 (v2,v4). Two fresh boundary halfedges with no face on them yet. The fix is to route the boundary loop through them instead of h3: h7 should lead into h13, h13 into h11, and h11 back into h1. Three pointer writes, and the walk closes again – h1 -> h9 -> h7 -> h13 -> h11 -> h1 – with the boundary now going around the new triangle.

sentences.en: The new edges brought not only h10 and h12 – the halfedges the face itself consumes – but also their twins: h11 (v4,v1) and h13 (v2,v4).
sentences.zh: 新边不仅带来了 h10 和 h12（面本身消耗的半边），还带来了它们的双胞胎：h11 (v4,v1) 和 h13 (v2,v4)。

sentences.en: Two fresh boundary halfedges with no face on them yet.
sentences.zh: 两个新的边界半边，上面还没有面。

sentences.en: The fix is to route the boundary loop through them instead of h3: h7 should lead into h13, h13 into h11, and h11 back into h1.
sentences.zh: 解决方法是通过它们而不是 h3 路由边界循环：h7 应该通向 h13，h13 通向 h11，h11 通向 h1。

sentences.en: Three pointer writes, and the walk closes again – h1 -> h9 -> h7 -> h13 -> h11 -> h1 – with the boundary now going around the new triangle.
sentences.zh: 三指针写入，步行再次关闭 - h1 -> h9 -> h7 -> h13 -> h11 -> h1 - 边界现在围绕新三角形。

blocks.html_en: Now add Face 2 using the existing edge E1 and the newly created edges E5 and E6, and update the affected halfedge links.

sentences.en: Now add Face 2 using the existing edge E1 and the newly created edges E5 and E6, and update the affected halfedge links.
sentences.zh: 现在使用现有边 E1 和新创建的边 E5 和 E6 添加面 2，并更新受影响的半边链接。

blocks.html_en: The invariant of the structure is that next(h) must be the next halfedge in the same cycle – either a face contour or a boundary loop – and it must start where h ends.

sentences.en: The invariant of the structure is that next(h) must be the next halfedge in the same cycle – either a face contour or a boundary loop – and it must start where h ends.
sentences.zh: 该结构的不变性是 next(h) 必须是同一循环中的下一个半边（无论是面部轮廓还是边界循环），并且它必须从 h 结束的地方开始。

sentences.zh: 替换实际句柄可得出：

blocks.html_en: These are exactly the same links that the general fix_next algorithm found by rotating around the affected vertices.

sentences.en: These are exactly the same links that the general fix_next algorithm found by rotating around the affected vertices.
sentences.zh: 这些与一般的 fix_next 算法通过围绕受影响的顶点旋转找到的链接完全相同。

blocks.html_en: There is one important catch: prev(h3) and next(h3) must be read before the old boundary links are overwritten.

sentences.en: There is one important catch: prev(h3) and next(h3) must be read before the old boundary links are overwritten.
sentences.zh: 有一个重要的问题：必须在覆盖旧边界链接之前读取 prev(h3) 和 next(h3)。

blocks.html_en: For this reason, add_face does not apply each change immediately. Instead, it stores the planned assignments in next_cache_:

sentences.en: For this reason, add_face does not apply each change immediately.
sentences.zh: 因此，add_face 不会立即应用每个更改。

sentences.en: Instead, it stores the planned assignments in next_cache_:
sentences.zh: 相反，它将计划的分配存储在 next_cache_ 中：

blocks.html_en: Only after all old links have been inspected does OpenMesh apply the entire batch:

sentences.en: Only after all old links have been inspected does OpenMesh apply the entire batch:
sentences.zh: 只有在检查完所有旧链接后，OpenMesh 才会应用整个批次：

blocks.html_en: Conceptually, fix_next rebuilds boundary connectivity by searching:

sentences.en: Conceptually, fix_next rebuilds boundary connectivity by searching:
sentences.zh: 从概念上讲，fix_next 通过搜索来重建边界连接：

blocks.html_en: OpenMesh usually reaches the same result through local splice operations:

sentences.en: OpenMesh usually reaches the same result through local splice operations:
sentences.zh: OpenMesh通常通过本地拼接操作达到相同的结果：

blocks.html_en: The more complicated re-link patches branch handles a different situation: two consecutive sides of the new face already exist as boundary halfedges, but they are not consecutive in the current boundary loop.

sentences.en: The more complicated re-link patches branch handles a different situation: two consecutive sides of the new face already exist as boundary halfedges, but they are not consecutive in the current boundary loop.
sentences.zh: 更复杂的重新链接补丁分支处理不同的情况：新面的两个连续边已经作为边界半边存在，但它们在当前边界循环中不连续。

blocks.html_en: In that case, a whole boundary patch may lie between them. OpenMesh rotates around the shared vertex to find another free boundary gap, moves the patch into that gap, and then links the two reused halfedges directly.

sentences.en: In that case, a whole boundary patch may lie between them.
sentences.zh: 在这种情况下，整个边界块可能位于它们之间。

sentences.en: OpenMesh rotates around the shared vertex to find another free boundary gap, moves the patch into that gap, and then links the two reused halfedges directly.
sentences.zh: OpenMesh 围绕共享顶点旋转以找到另一个自由边界间隙，将面片移动到该间隙中，然后直接链接两个重用的半边。

blocks.html_en: This rotation follows the same underlying boundary invariant as fix_next, but it performs a larger patch-splicing operation rather than repairing only one next pointer.

sentences.en: This rotation follows the same underlying boundary invariant as fix_next, but it performs a larger patch-splicing operation rather than repairing only one next pointer.
sentences.zh: 这种旋转遵循与 fix_next 相同的底层边界不变量，但它执行更大的补丁拼接操作，而不是仅修复一个下一个指针。

blocks.html_en: Why adjust_outgoing_halfedge() is still needed

sentences.en: Why adjust_outgoing_halfedge() is still needed
sentences.zh: 为什么仍然需要 adjustment_outgoing_halfedge()

blocks.html_en: At the end, adjust_outgoing_halfedge() checks whether a vertex still points to a suitable outgoing halfedge. After adding a face, the halfedge stored by a boundary vertex may now belong to the new face and no longer be part of the boundary. The function rotates around the vertex, finds another outgoing boundary halfedge if one exists, and stores it as the vertex’s new representative. It does not change the mesh topology – it only fixes the vertex’s entry point for future traversal.

sentences.en: At the end, adjust_outgoing_halfedge() checks whether a vertex still points to a suitable outgoing halfedge.
sentences.zh: 最后，调整_outgoing_halfedge() 检查顶点是否仍然指向合适的传出半边。

sentences.en: After adding a face, the halfedge stored by a boundary vertex may now belong to the new face and no longer be part of the boundary.
sentences.zh: 添加面后，边界顶点存储的半边现在可能属于新面，而不再是边界的一部分。

sentences.en: The function rotates around the vertex, finds another outgoing boundary halfedge if one exists, and stores it as the vertex’s new representative.
sentences.zh: 该函数围绕顶点旋转，找到另一个传出边界半边（如果存在），并将其存储为顶点的新代表。

sentences.en: It does not change the mesh topology – it only fixes the vertex’s entry point for future traversal.
sentences.zh: 它不会改变网格拓扑——它只是修复了未来遍历的顶点入口点。

blocks.html_en: Summary

sentences.en: Summary
sentences.zh: 概括

blocks.html_en: Conceptually, the function performs three main jobs:

sentences.en: Conceptually, the function performs three main jobs:
sentences.zh: 从概念上讲，该函数执行三项主要工作：

blocks.html_en: The conceptual fix_next rule explains which boundary halfedges must be connected. OpenMesh usually avoids performing the full rotational search and instead computes the same links directly from the old boundary structure. It stores those changes in next_cache_, applies them only after all required reads are complete, and finally calls adjust_outgoing_halfedge() for any vertex whose stored traversal entry point is no longer suitable.

sentences.en: The conceptual fix_next rule explains which boundary halfedges must be connected.
sentences.zh: 概念性的 fix_next 规则解释了必须连接哪些边界半边。

sentences.en: OpenMesh usually avoids performing the full rotational search and instead computes the same links directly from the old boundary structure.
sentences.zh: OpenMesh 通常避免执行完整的旋转搜索，而是直接从旧的边界结构计算相同的链接。

sentences.en: It stores those changes in next_cache_, applies them only after all required reads are complete, and finally calls adjust_outgoing_halfedge() for any vertex whose stored traversal entry point is no longer suitable.
sentences.zh: 它将这些更改存储在 next_cache_ 中，仅在所有必需的读取完成后应用它们，最后对存储的遍历入口点不再合适的任何顶点调用 adjustment_outgoing_halfedge() 。

content_html_zh:
在上一篇文章中，我们介绍了半边数据结构的基础知识以及如何使用 OpenMesh 遍历其元素。 这次，我们将深入了解 add_face 的底层——该函数动态构建整个结构。 当您致电时：

OpenMesh 执行一系列操作来连接整个拓扑图。 让我们一步步追踪到底发生了什么。

想象两个三角形形成一个四边形，第三个三角形连接到其右侧。 让我们从第 1 篇文章中描述的简单四边形开始。

并添加第三个三角形连接到其右侧。

在添加 F2 之前，四边形的边界环是闭合的并且包含四个半边：

h1(v1, v0)
-> h9(v0, v3)
-> h7(v3, v2)
-> h3(v2, v1)
-> h1

现在观察如果我们添加 F2 而不更新边界链接会发生什么。

假设我们创建两条缺失的边——四个新的半边，h10 到 h13——分配新面，并闭合其内部轮廓。 现有的 halfedge h3 现在延续到 h10。

再次尝试沿着旧边界行走：

h1(v1, v0)
-> h9(v0, v3)
-> h7(v3, v2)
-> h3(v2, v1)
-> h10(v1, v4)
-> h12(v4, v2)
-> h3
-> h10
-> ...

步行永远不会返回到 h1。

我们穿过 h3 进入新的面孔并永远围绕那里运行。 边界环被破坏。

因此，添加面不仅仅是创建缺失边缘的问题。 接缝周围的半边链接也必须按照正确的顺序仔细修复。 这就是 add_face() 内部大部分工作的内容。

新边不仅带来了 h10 和 h12（面本身消耗的半边），还带来了它们的双胞胎：h11 (v4,v1) 和 h13 (v2,v4)。 两个新的边界半边，上面还没有面。 解决方法是通过它们而不是 h3 路由边界循环：h7 应该通向 h13，h13 通向 h11，h11 通向 h1。 三指针写入，步行再次关闭 - h1 -> h9 -> h7 -> h13 -> h11 -> h1 - 边界现在围绕新三角形。

现在使用现有边 E1 和新创建的边 E5 和 E6 添加面 2，并更新受影响的半边链接。

接下来计算

但是我们如何计算这些下一个指针呢？

该结构的不变性是 next(h) 必须是同一循环中的下一个半边（无论是面部轮廓还是边界循环），并且它必须从 h 结束的地方开始。

因此，要修复边界半边，我们可以站在其末端顶点并旋转传出的半边，直到找到另一个边界：

在运行该算法之前，新脸部的内部轮廓必须已经有效：

并且所有三个半边都必须属于 F2。

现在运行 h7 的 fix_next，它在 v2 处结束。

v2 处的传出半边按旋转顺序为：

搜索开始于：

由于 h6 属于 F1，因此继续：

h4属于F0，所以再次旋转：

但 h3 现在属于新添加的面 F2，因此旋转再次继续：

h13 是边界半边，因此搜索停止：

注意发生了什么变化。 在添加 F2 之前，h3 仍然是边界半边，因此旋转停在那里：

新面孔消耗了旧边界半边。 现在相同的旋转穿过它并到达下一个自由的传出半边 h13。

同样的规则产生另外两个边界链接。

对于以新顶点 v4 结束的 h13，第一个传出候选者是：

h12 属于 F2，因此旋转一圈给出：

h11 是边界，因此：

新顶点 v4 处只有两个传出半边，即 h12 和 h11，因此旋转只是穿过内部半边到达外部半边。

最后，考虑 h11，它以 v1 结束。

旋转开始于：

h10 属于 F2，所以下一个候选是：

h2属于F0，所以我们再次旋转：

h1 是边界，给出：

因此，所有三个必需的写入都遵循相同的一般规则：

修复后的边界环为：

OpenMesh 解决方案

对于这种常见情况，OpenMesh 不需要执行这些旋转。 答案已经编码在旧的边界循环中。

在插入 F2 之前，现有的半边 h3 是边界的一部分：

所以：

当 h3 成为新面的一部分时，它的两个新边界替换已经已知：

因此，OpenMesh 可以使用直接指针操作构造新边界：

替换实际句柄可得出：

这些与一般的 fix_next 算法通过围绕受影响的顶点旋转找到的链接完全相同。

有一个重要的问题：必须在覆盖旧边界链接之前读取 prev(h3) 和 next(h3)。

因此，add_face 不会立即应用每个更改。 相反，它将计划的分配存储在 next_cache_ 中：

只有在检查完所有旧链接后，OpenMesh 才会应用整个批次：

从概念上讲，fix_next 通过搜索来重建边界连接：

OpenMesh通常通过本地拼接操作达到相同的结果：

更复杂的重新链接补丁分支处理不同的情况：新面的两个连续边已经作为边界半边存在，但它们在当前边界循环中不连续。

在这种情况下，整个边界块可能位于它们之间。 OpenMesh 围绕共享顶点旋转以找到另一个自由边界间隙，将面片移动到该间隙中，然后直接链接两个重用的半边。

这种旋转遵循与 fix_next 相同的底层边界不变量，但它执行更大的补丁拼接操作，而不是仅修复一个下一个指针。

为什么仍然需要 adjustment_outgoing_halfedge()

最后，调整_outgoing_halfedge() 检查顶点是否仍然指向合适的传出半边。 添加面后，边界顶点存储的半边现在可能属于新面，而不再是边界的一部分。 该函数围绕顶点旋转，找到另一个传出边界半边（如果存在），并将其存储为顶点的新代表。 它不会改变网格拓扑——它只是修复了未来遍历的顶点入口点。

概括

从概念上讲，该函数执行三项主要工作：

概念性的 fix_next 规则解释了必须连接哪些边界半边。 OpenMesh 通常避免执行完整的旋转搜索，而是直接从旧的边界结构计算相同的链接。 它将这些更改存储在 next_cache_ 中，仅在所有必需的读取完成后应用它们，最后对存储的遍历入口点不再合适的任何顶点调用 adjustment_outgoing_halfedge() 。
